subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $A$ be the area, $b$ be the breadth and $\ell$ be the length of the rectangle.
Given: $\frac{d A}{d t}=-5, \frac{d \ell}{d t}=2, \frac{d b}{d t}=-3$
We know, $A=\ell \times b$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d t}=\ell \cdot \frac{d b}{d t}+b \cdot \frac{d \ell}{d t}=-3 \ell+2 b \\
& \Rightarrow-5=-3 \ell+2 b .
\end{aligned}
$$
When $b=2$, we have
$$
-5=-3 \ell+4 \Rightarrow \ell=\frac{9}{3}=3 m
$$

$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \gt 0 \forall(-\infty,-3) \cup(3, \infty) \rightarrow \text { increasing } \\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \lt 0 \forall(-3,0) \cup(0,3) \rightarrow \text { decreasing }\end{aligned}$

& \Rightarrow c=\frac{2}{\log _e 3}=2 \log _3 e.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let base $=b$
Altitude (or perpendicular) $=\sqrt{h^2-b^2}$ Area, $\mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{2} \times$ base $\times$ altitude $=\frac{1}{2} \times b \times \sqrt{h^2-b^2}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{d \mathrm{~A}}{d b}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\sqrt{h^2-b^2}+b \cdot \frac{-2 b}{2 \sqrt{h^2-b^2}}\right]$ $=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{h^2-2 b^2}{\sqrt{h^2-b^2}}\right]$ Put $\frac{d \mathrm{~A}}{d b}=0, \Rightarrow b=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}$ Maximumarea $=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{h}{\sqrt{2}} \times \sqrt{h^2-\frac{h^2}{2}}=\frac{h^2}{4}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let P be the nearest to 32, 0, then normal at P will pass through 32, 0.

Any point on the parabola y2=4ax can be taken as at2, 2at

Let, co-ordinates of P on the curve y=x, ⇒y2=x can be taken as t24, t2


The equation of the normal to the parabola y2=4ax at a point at2, 2at is y+tx=2at+at3.

Hence, the equation of normal to y=x or y2=x at t24, t2 is y+tx=t2+t34

This line passes through 32, 0, hence 
3t2=t2+t34

⇒t3-4t=0

⇒tt2-4=0

⇒t=0, 2, -2 but -2 is rejected as we have to take point in the first quadrant.

Thus, the points are 0, 0 or 1, 1 and the distances of these points from 32, 0 are respectively

32-02+0-02=32 and 32-12+1-02=52 units.

Hence, nearest point is 1,1 and the minimum distance is 52 units.

solution:

On differentiating both sides, we get

12x2-3y2-6xyy'+12x-5y-5xy'-16yy'+9=0

Now, at the point -2,3

48-27+36y'-24-15+10y'-48y'+9=0

i.e. y'-2,3=-92

So slope of tangent  mT=-92 and 

slope of normal mN=29

i.e. equation of tangent T≡y-3=-92x+2⇒y=-92x-6

and equation of normal N≡y-3=29x+2⇒y=29x+319

Intersection point of tangent and normal is -2,3

The intersection point of tangent and normal with x-axis respectively will be -43,0 & -312,0

Now, area of triangle formed =12×Base×Height

A=12×-43+3123=12856·3=854

⇒8A=170

Let fx=ax4+bx3+cx2+dx+e

Then limx→01+fxx2=3

⇒limx→01+ax2+bx+c+dx+ex2=3

This limit exists when d=e=0.

So,  limx→01+ax2+bx+c=3

⇒c=2

It is given, x=1 and x=2 are the solution of f'x=0.

⇒x4ax2+3bx+2c=0

⇒b=-4a

Product of roots =2c4a=1·2=2

⇒a=c4

Now, Let AB =x, BD=y

Now given area of triangle ∆CAB=23-3

Now finding,

tanθ2=4x3-yx=43-(43-6)x2

tanθ1=yx=43-6x2

And cos60°=CDCE⇒CE=2x=6-23

$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=-\left(p^2-6 p+8\right) \cos 4 n+2(2-p) n+7 \\ & f^1(x)=+4\left(p^2-6 p+8\right) \sin 4 x+(4-2 p) \neq 0 \\ & \sin 4 x \neq \frac{2 p-4}{4(p-4)(p-2)}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \sin 4 x \neq \frac{2(p-2)}{4(p-4)(p-2)} \\
& p \neq 2 \\
& \quad \sin 4 x \neq \frac{1}{2(p-4)} \\
& \Rightarrow\left|\frac{1}{2(p-4)}\right|>1
\end{aligned}$
on solving we get
$\therefore \mathrm{p} \in\left(\frac{7}{2}, \frac{9}{2}\right)$
Hence $\mathrm{a}=\frac{7}{2}, \mathrm{~b}=\frac{9}{2}$
$\therefore 16 \mathrm{ab}=252$

$f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+(\sin x+\cos x)^2}(\cos x-\sin x)$
$=\frac{\sqrt{2} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{1+(\sin x+\cos x)^2}$
$f(x)$ is increasing if $-\frac{\pi}{2} < x+\frac{\pi}{4} < \frac{\pi}{2}$
$-\frac{3 \pi}{4} < x < \frac{\pi}{4}$
hence $f(x)$ is increasing when $x \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$.

subject: Mathematics
options: it is at a constant distance from the origin
solution:
Clearly $\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow$ slope of normal $=-\cot \theta$
Equation of normal at ' $\theta$ ' is
$y-a(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta)=-\cot \theta(x-a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta)$
$\Rightarrow y \sin \theta-a \sin ^2 \theta+a \theta \cos \theta \sin \theta=-x \cos \theta+a \cos ^2 \theta+a \theta \sin \theta \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow x \cos \theta+y \sin \theta=a$
Clearly this is an equation of straight line which is at a constant distance 'a' from origin.

solution: Let $f^{\prime}(x)=a x^2+b x+c \Rightarrow f(x)=\frac{a x^3}{3}+\frac{b x^2}{2}+c x+d$ $\Rightarrow f(x)=\frac{1}{6}\left(2 a x^3+3 b x^2+6 c x+6 d\right)$, Now $f(1)=f(0)=d$, then according to Rolle's theorem $\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=a x^2+b x+c=0$ has at least one root in $(0,1)$

solution:
fx=ax5+bx4+cx3

Consider, limx→0⁡2+ax5+bx4+cx3x3=4

⇒2+c=4

⇒c=2

Differentiating fx with respect to x

f'x=5ax4+4bx3+6x2

=x25ax2+4bx+6

Critical points of the function are given as ±1.

f'1=0⇒5a+4b+6=0

f'-1=0⇒5a-4b+6=0
Solving both we get, b=0, a=-65
So, fx=-65x5+2x3
And f'x=-6x4+6x2

=-6x2x+1x-1

Hence, local minima at x=-1 and local maxima at x=1.

solution:
Given,

y5-9xy+2x=0 .......1

Now differentiating both side we get,

5y4dyx-9xdydx-9y+2=0

⇒dydx5y4-9x=9y-2

⇒dydx=9y-25y4-9x=0 .....2 

⇒9y-2=0 (for horizontal tangent)

⇒y=29, which does not satisfy the original equation so M=0.

As 295-9x×29+2x=0

⇒295-2x+2x=0

⇒295=0 which is a false statement 

Now 5y4-9x=0  (from equation 2 for vertical tangent)

⇒5y49y-2-9y5=0 (putting the value of x from equation 1)

⇒y445y-10-9y=0

⇒y=0 or 36y=10⇒y=518

So at y=0⇒x=0 and y=518⇒x=x1

0,0x1,518 (where x1=51859×518-2)

So, N=2

M+N=0+2=2

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Equation of normal is $Y-y=-\frac{d x}{d y}(X-x)$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow G \equiv\left(x+y \frac{d y}{d x}, 0\right) \\
& \left|x+y \frac{d y}{d x}\right|=|2 x| \\
& \Rightarrow y \frac{d y}{d x}=x \text { or } y \frac{d y}{d x}=-3 x \\
& y d y=x d x \text { or } y d y=-3 x d x \\
& \frac{y^2}{2}=\frac{x^2}{2}+c \text { or } \frac{y^2}{2}=-\frac{3 x^2}{2}+c \\
& x^2-y^2=-2 c \text { or } 3 x^2+y^2=2 c.
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $\operatorname{cost} \mathrm{C}=a v+\frac{b}{v}$
According to given question,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 30 a+\frac{b}{30}=75 \\
& 40 a+\frac{b}{40}=65
\end{aligned}
$$
On solving (i) and (ii), we get $a=\frac{1}{2}$ and $b=1800$
Now, $C=a v+\frac{b}{v}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{d C}{d v}=a-\frac{b}{v^2} \\
& \frac{d C}{d v}=0 \Rightarrow a-\frac{b}{v^2}=0 \\
& \Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\frac{b}{a}}=\sqrt{3600} \\
& \Rightarrow v=60 \mathrm{kmph}
\end{aligned}
$$

solution:
Let fx=x6+ax5+bx4+cx3+dx2+ex+f

as limx→0fxx3=1 non-zero finite

So, d=e=f=0

and fx=x3x3+ax2+bx+c

Hence, limx→0fxx3=c=1

Now, as fx=x6+ax5+bx4+x3

and f'x=0 at x=1 and x=-1

i.e., f'x=6x5+5ax4+4bx3+3x2

f'1=0

⇒6+5a+4b+3=0

⇒5a+4b=-9

and f'-1=0

⇒-6+5a-4b+3=0

⇒5a-4b=3

Solving both we get,

a=-610=-35; b=-32

∴ fx=x6-35x5-32x4+x3

∴ 5f2=564-35·32-32·16+8

=320-96-120+40

=144
